task: I’m dealing with a contractor who wants to change the terms after the job is already underway, and I don’t want to get pushed around. Can you help me figure out what to say, what to put in writing, and when it’s smarter to stop and get another opinion?

turns.agent_response:
Yes. Here’s a short, practical approach you can use right now.

## 1) What to look for
Check whether the new term is:
- **Already in the original proposal/contract**
- **Covered by a signed change order**
- **Supported by a dated, written approval**
- **Matched by the invoice/job notes**

Circle these phrases if you see them:
- **automatically**
- **unless**
- **subject to**
- **deemed**
- **incorporated by reference**
- **payment constitutes acceptance**
- **in the event of a conflict**

Also check:
- price changes
- extra work claims
- cancellation/termination language
- renewal or follow-on work buried in attachments
- license number and license name, if it’s a service job

## 2) What to put in writing
Ask for a **line-by-line correction document** showing:
- what was originally agreed
- what is now being claimed as changed
- the date each change was requested
- who approved it
- the sign-off for each item

Use wording like this:

> I’m not approving any change that is not matched to the original agreement, proposal, or a dated written authorization.  
> Please send a line-by-line correction document showing each alleged change, the date requested, the approving party, and the signed authorization.  
> Until that is provided, I am withholding payment for the disputed items only.

If they say “extra work,” ask:

> Please identify the exact extra work, the prior written approval for it, the rate or amount approved, and the date/sign-off.

If it’s a service job:

> Please also provide the license number, the name on the license, and any signed authorizations for add-ons.

## 3) What to pay now vs hold back
### Safe to pay only if verified:
- items clearly listed in the original agreement
- charges backed by a signed change order
- undisputed completed work at the original rate

### Hold back:
- any new charge with no prior written approval
- rate increases announced after the fact
- “extra work” with no dated sign-off
- items that don’t match the proposal or job scope
- anything that depends on vague language like “subject to” or “deemed”

## 4) When to stop and get another opinion
Bring in another opinion if:
- the dispute is over **$10,000**
- the contract language is dense or tricky
- they keep changing the story
- there’s a history of billing problems
- they refuse to produce written approval
- you feel pressured or outmatched

## 5) A firm next-step note you can send
> I’m reviewing the job against the original proposal and any signed change orders.  
> I will pay only the charges that match a prior agreement or dated written authorization.  
> Please send a line-by-line correction document for every item you say was changed or added, with dates, sign-offs, and supporting documentation.  
> Until then, I am holding the disputed charges.

If you want, paste the contractor’s message or invoice text and I’ll help you mark up exactly what to challenge.

user_simulator.text:
Mostly right. But I’d want it sharper. Don’t give them a whole essay. One clean written notice, and no arguing on the phone.

What I’d say:

> I’m not agreeing to any change unless it’s in the original contract or a dated written change order signed by me. Send the exact itemized changes, the date each one was requested, who approved it, and the written authorization. Until that’s provided, I’m disputing the added charges and withholding payment on those items only.

And if they keep pushing, add:

> If you can’t document the change, assume it was never approved.

What I’d put in writing:
- copy of the original contract
- any change order
- invoice
- photos if the work is claimed to be “extra”
- names, dates, and phone calls logged

When to stop and get another opinion? Sooner than they said, if the story starts shifting. If they say one thing on the phone and another on paper, that’s enough for me to pause. I’ve dealt with that kind of nonsense before. Once they start inventing extras mid-job, it usually doesn’t get better.

If you want, I can help you draft a tighter note you can actually send without sounding emotional.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter version that fits your style and keeps it in writing only.

### Short written notice to send
> I do not agree to any change unless it is in the original contract or in a dated written change order signed by me.  
> Send the exact itemized changes, the date each was requested, who approved each one, and the written authorization.  
> Until that is provided, I dispute the added charges and withhold payment on those items only.

### If they keep pushing
> If you cannot document the change, I will treat it as unapproved.

### What to save in writing
Keep:
- original contract/proposal
- all change orders
- invoice(s)
- photos if they claim “extra work”
- texts/emails
- a log of names, dates, and what was said

### When to stop and get another opinion
Pause and get another opinion if:
- the story changes from phone to paper
- they won’t give written backup
- the extra charges appear after work started
- the amount is significant
- they keep pressuring you instead of documenting it

### Best rule
Don’t argue on the phone.  
Just say:
> Put it in writing.

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-paragraph dispute notice** you can copy and send today.

user_simulator.text:
Better. That’s the right shape — short, written, no phone theater. I’d use this.

One small tweak: I’d drop “I will treat it as unapproved” and make it plainer.

> I do not agree to any change unless it is in the original contract or in a dated written change order signed by me. Send the exact itemized changes, the date each was requested, who approved each one, and the written authorization. Until that is provided, I dispute the added charges and withhold payment on those items only.

And yes, keep the paper trail:
- original contract/proposal
- change orders
- invoice(s)
- photos if they claim extra work
- emails/texts
- names, dates, and what was said

If the story shifts between phone and paper, I’d stop there too. That’s usually enough of a warning sign. I’ve seen that game before — once they start inventing extras, it turns into a mess.

So, yes. No arguing. Put it in writing. Keep it tight.
